author: @oldboy5001
text: Not sure about the comment saying it's too heavy compared to the Halfords bog standard. Aren't all ebikes around 25+ kilos? And surely the weight difference is more than compensated for by the power.

author: @This1sEngland
text: Hi David, I own the Eskute Netuno of which I gave your brother Robert and his friend a go of at Broom Park August 22. On reference to the battery range diminishing, Try this. Plan a route so your battery totally drains and turns off the bike. When you get home charge the battery for a full 8 hrs, This will balance the cells to all equal amounts and you will get your range back. I have owned my netuno for 14 months now and I actually get 65 miles on 3 assist. I also have the battery fully charged for all journeys as I do not intend to be storing the battery fully charged for any period. Plus a fully charged battery gives you more power, acceleration and an effort free ride. Hope this helps. :)

replies.author: @oldboy5001
replies.text: Do you mean you recharge the battery to 100% even after short trips ?  Is that okay for the battery? Doesn't it need to discharge fully before recharging? Just curious because I've ordered one of these.

author: @rickwhite7736
text: 4 years ago I converted my £30 mountain bike to electric using a 500 watt front wheel and a 15 amp hour battery which I bought off eBay for £350. It has a throttle as well as pedal assist and can do 34 miles with a full charge and still can 4 years later. Best thing I every did with a bike it just make every hill flat and I love it. I have done about 4,000 miles so far. It does make the bike a bit heavier but you get used to that.

author: @thehazelnutspread
text: I'd be worried about the batteries bursting into flames and would want to store it in a fireproof shed.

author: @billmmckelvie5188
text: Thanks for the long term review. The performance comparison between road and electric bikes is just a bit worse than I expected. As I am doing a double the commute the standard road bike is looking the better option in my opinion from a cost benefit analysis!

author: @Electrowave
text: We do need more of these reviews, done after a decent period of ownership. I just bought an e-bike myself, and hoping for a decent lifespan with the huge cost of it. The battery is my biggest worry because a new one is currently nearly £500! If it lasts long enough then I will probably save that amount on petrol for the car. That's the theory 🙂

author: @sarchlalaith8836
text: Give it back to Rob to upgrade to an aluminium frame and do all the crazy things only Rob would ;)

replies.author: @markyprescott202
replies.text: And check and test each and every battery from the battery pack,  18650 (rechargeable batteries are only as strong as it's weakest battery) batteries are used in laptops, power tools, camera's etc, replace the weakest and you are back to as good as full power.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: I can remember as a kid, biking about 40 miles a day all summer with a friend, just to see these girls about 20 miles  away. No electric bikes back then. One time even biking 160 miles round trip in two days just because I wanted to do this personal goal of mine.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: Me/us too, never off our bikes (not the girls  ;-) ), Ride Sheffield/Castleton,  Sheffield/Matlock, Sheffield/Buxton nearly every week-end; Sheffield/York, Sheffield/Whitby, Sheffield/Penrith, Sheffield/Yarmouth etc several times a year. From 12-50 years old. Still regularly do Sheffield/Castleton-Buxton etc at 66-years old.

author: @MosquitoWarspite
text: It seems to me that your brother might be better clipping an E bike converter to his Boardman, for those hilly journeys.

author: @rhiantaylor3446
text: Looking up the specs, the bike has a 522wh battery so if you are happy that it achieves 65mile range, that means you are using only 8wh per mile which is very good - well done ! I commonly see manufacturer claims that imply 10 to 20 wh/mile depending on how much effort you are contributing through the pedals and of course the nature of the terrain. With electrified mopeds which have no pedalling option at all, I have seen a figure of 25wh/mile quoted.

text: Great review!

author: @kadmow
text: e-bike range - even on well graded flat trails at  "reasonable speeds", is heavily dependent on the amount of pedalling the rider supplies - as well as the weight of the rider somewhat).

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Never heard of it.

I've put 4700 miles on a normal 10 speed, got super stacked, people thought I was a body builder or a pro wrestler...

CAIN'T NEVER get THAT from a TOY!!!

replies.author: @ravenmad9225
replies.text: Pro riders have bigger legs but are super skinny ,so you must have done some lifting to get like that.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@ravenmad9225 Nope.

Never.

You can't smash me inside of a gym with a hydraulic ram.

[except that one time I worked on a construction company BUILDING one, but I was long gone before that one ever opened its doors!]

To this day I mock weight lifters.

Always shall too.

Those who lift lack the gift.

Us Bunneh Wabbutz were born to move and move fast, not sit some place with a bunch of sweaty dudes with personality disorders.

I got ALL my size from cycling...

Except maybe for some occasional concrete or steel beam jobs, but those were few and far between due to an effed up economy that left me with way too much free time to tour the world on a cycle.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@ravenmad9225 Also...

I'm NOT a 'pro' rider.

Pro riders are looking for something other than what I was going for.

I went for hard core sheer speed while climbing hills [up hill drag racing] so I could get explosive power which also helps with kicks and leaps in Kung Fu.

I developed legs with solid enduring power by putting on many a mile, but also, I developed legs capable of snapping fast and hard with all their power at an opponent with the intentions of dealing maximum damage no matter how many times I had to strike them, and no many how many of them I had to strike.

author: @lightcapmath2777
text: Could not find your brothers link? DVD:)

author: @Istandby666
text:
I designed and built my own ebike.
I could do 36 mph (max). Both front and rear tires had a 1,000 watt hub. I could go 25 miles on a charge. I charged it every night. It was my main transportation to and from work.
I installed the non flat foam inside my tire's, because when I'd ride over railroad tracks at 36 mph, it would pop my tire's. I rode at least 2,500 miles/ year.
Obviously I would ride it on nice day's, no rain, no snow.
I built my bike back in 2019, I still have it. I've only replaced the battery, brake's, and tire's.

author: @lorenbush8876
text: I'm glad your brother is loving the bike riding, it surely is cheaper, I'll check his channel out.. My bike is a k-mart return that I paid $5 for and I don't remember if I had to do anything to it to make it rideable or not but later I converted it to electric. I  I ride mine a lot and I've had I've it over 2 years, I don't think I've put that many miles on it though, mine will only go 10 miles or so on a charge, I don't try to ride in the rain or on ice or thawed and frozen snow, I have ridden on snow I just have to be careful. I use my truck when I need to get large things or a lot of things.

author: @mark.guitar
text: Time to make a drive assist for your brother. 6 months to Christmas so not unreasonable! use a "bottle motor" to drive the front wheel maybe?

author: @JSabh
text: Nice to meet your brother, it is awesome that you guys are sharing content like this. I built an ebike from an old Trek and put a 1400 watt motor on with about 40 ah at 48 V for battery. I rode it over 1800 miles up very steep hills and it was the finest part of my day. I would find excuses just to ride and fly on it. It did go 45 MPH though lol so there is that. I built it for the sailboat and exploring islands, absolutely incredible fun and it would go a good 65 to 110 miles depending on if I peddled or not. You have motivated the third iteration of my ebike journey btw. All in all, it cost around 1300 US dollars to build and I built it in less than 5 hours.

replies.text: Thanks for sharing. I can't afford that, so I've been wondering about how to use a battery operated drill with my bicycle somehow. I saw a kid had a weedwacker powered wheelchair zipping down the road Made me laugh &wonder what I could do..❤
